title: Detecting Cycles in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

statement.markdown: Given a DAG represented as an adjacency list, implement a cycle detection function. The function should return `True` if there's a cycle and `False` otherwise.
statement.inputFormat: Input will be provided as adjacency list where each index represents a node and the corresponding value is a list of its children.
statement.outputFormat: Return `True` if there's a cycle and `False` otherwise.
statement.notes: The graph contains at least one node and all edges are directed. It's guaranteed that the input graph is a DAG unless a cycle exists.

constraints.description: Number of nodes in the graph

examples.explanation: No cycle exists in the graph

hints.text: Think about a way to detect the cycle in a graph using DFS.

editorial.title: Cycle Detection in DAG
editorial.intuition: We can use a recursive DFS function to detect the cycle in the graph.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute force approach is to check all possible pairs of nodes and verify if there's a path connecting them, which would indicate the existence of a cycle.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a recursive DFS function to detect the cycle in the graph.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can maintain a `visited` set to keep track of visited nodes and a `recStack` set to keep track of the current recursion stack.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of this algorithm can be proven using a mathematical induction argument.
editorial.dryRun: Suppose we have a graph represented as an adjacency list. We can start a DFS traversal from an arbitrary node and keep track of visited and recursion stack.
commonMistakes: Not keeping track of recursion stack
